ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for kimchi chicken yakitori bowl. FOODON_00002809 (chicken dish) used as closest parent. A more specific donburi or yakitori bowl term is not available in FoodOn.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP (chicken meat dish) used as closest parent. No specific yakitori bowl or kimchi chicken donburi code exists in FoodEx2 at this time.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT clinical concept maps to a kimchi chicken yakitori bowl dish. SNOMED CT does not catalogue composite restaurant dishes at this specificity.

serving.description: 1 bowl combo (rice bowl + kimchi + half-boiled egg + miso soup or drink)
serving.natural_language_portion: One serving is approximately 550 g, comprising a rice bowl with grilled chicken yakitori (approximately 200 g cooked rice, 120 g grilled chicken), 80 g kimchi, one half-boiled egg (~50 g), and miso soup (~100 g), as typically served in a Japanese casual dining combo format in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data: Chicken, broilers or fryers, thigh, meat only, cooked, roasted (FDC ID 171477); Rice, white, short-grain, cooked (FDC ID 169760); Kimchi (FDC ID 169999); Egg, whole, cooked, hard-boiled (FDC ID 173424); Miso soup, prepared (FDC ID 174246); Soy-based tare sauce estimated from soy sauce (FDC ID 172238)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for kimchi chicken yakitori bowl combo. Nutrient values estimated by weighted component assembly using USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy and Foundation Foods entries for each major ingredient. Approximate component weights per 100 g of combined dish: cooked rice 36%, grilled chicken thigh 22%, kimchi 15%, half-boiled egg 9%, miso soup 16%, tare sauce 2%. Values represent a plausible central estimate; actual restaurant preparation may vary.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al.; supplemented by University of Sydney GI Database entry for white rice (cooked, short-grain, GI ~72) and mixed rice-based meal attenuation from protein and fat co-ingestion
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for kimchi chicken yakitori bowl. GI estimated at 56 (medium) based on: (1) white short-grain rice GI ~72 from University of Sydney GI Database; (2) attenuation by co-ingested protein (chicken ~22% of dish weight) and fat (~4.1 g/100 g) per published mixed-meal GI reduction literature (Atkinson et al. 2021, Am J Clin Nutr); (3) kimchi fermented vegetable component providing acetic acid which further lowers postprandial glucose response. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (56 × 97.9) / 100 ≈ 55.0, where carbohydrate per 550 g serving = 17.8 × 5.5 = 97.9 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from kimchi, tare soy glaze, and miso soup
hypertension.guidance: A single serving of this combo provides an estimated 2310 mg sodium (420 mg/100 g × 550 g), which exceeds the WHO recommended daily limit of 2000 mg; individuals with hypertension should request reduced-sodium tare sauce, limit miso soup consumption, and consider smaller portions of kimchi.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic index (~56) driven by white short-grain rice, partially attenuated by protein, fat, and fermented kimchi
type2_diabetes.guidance: The medium GI of approximately 56 and high glycemic load per serving (~55) indicate a meaningful postprandial glucose response; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consider reducing the rice portion, pairing with additional non-starchy vegetables, and monitoring blood glucose response, as the kimchi and protein content provide partial glycemic attenuation.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and moderate saturated fat from chicken and egg yolk, partially offset by lean grilled preparation
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The high sodium load (~2310 mg per serving) poses a significant cardiovascular risk for individuals with existing heart disease; however, the grilled (non-fried) chicken preparation and moderate saturated fat content (~6 g per serving) are relatively favourable compared to fried alternatives, and the kimchi may provide probiotic and anti-inflammatory benefits.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Kimchi Chicken Yakitori Bowl Combo is a Japanese-Korean fusion rice bowl dish served in casual Japanese dining restaurants in Singapore, notably at Sukiya and similar mix-and-match concepts. The dish centres on grilled chicken yakitori — chicken pieces (typically thigh) skewered and glazed with a sweet-savoury tare sauce made from soy sauce, mirin, and sake — served over a bed of steamed Japanese short-grain rice. The bowl is topped with kimchi, a Korean fermented napa cabbage condiment that provides a spicy, tangy, and umami-rich contrast to the mild sweetness of the yakitori glaze. The combo is typically served with a half-boiled egg (soft-cooked, with a runny yolk) and either miso soup or a beverage. The texture profile combines tender, slightly charred grilled chicken with soft sticky rice, crunchy fermented kimchi, and a creamy egg yolk. Nutritionally, the dish provides approximately 148 kcal per 100 g, with meaningful protein (~10 g/100 g) from chicken and egg, moderate carbohydrate from rice (~18 g/100 g), and relatively low fat (~4 g/100 g) due to the grilled rather than fried preparation. The primary nutritional concern is sodium, estimated at approximately 420 mg/100 g or over 2300 mg per full serving, driven by the tare glaze, kimchi, and miso soup. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 56 (medium), attenuated from the base white rice GI by co-ingested protein, fat, and the acetic acid in kimchi. This dish is suitable for individuals seeking a protein-rich, moderate-calorie Japanese meal but requires sodium management for those with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. It is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or those with soy or gluten allergies without modification.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for kimchi chicken yakitori bowl combo as a composite dish. Nutrient values were estimated by weighted component assembly from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy and Foundation Foods entries for each major ingredient (grilled chicken thigh, cooked short-grain rice, kimchi, hard-boiled egg, miso soup, soy-based tare sauce). GI was estimated by analogy from University of Sydney GI Database white rice data with documented mixed-meal attenuation. Confidence is medium because the component-assembly method introduces uncertainty in exact proportions and preparation-specific nutrient losses.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are component-assembly estimates using USDA FoodData Central approved sources; no HPB Singapore Foodo or FAO/INFOODS SEA entry was found for this specific dish. 2. Serving size of 550 g is an estimate based on typical Japanese casual dining combo portions in Singapore; actual restaurant serving weights may vary. 3. Sodium estimate is high due to cumulative contributions from tare sauce, kimchi, and miso soup; this is a clinically significant concern. 4. GI of 56 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for this composite dish was found in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature. 5. trans_fat_g is set to 0.0 as grilled chicken and rice-based dishes do not contain industrially produced trans fats; this is confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central component data. 6. The cuisine_tags in the input JSON include 'Beef Dish' and 'Japanese Curry' which are not applicable to this dish and have been disregarded; these appear to be platform-level category tags rather than dish-specific descriptors. 7. monounsaturated_fat_g (-1 sentinel not used; value 1.5 estimated from USDA chicken thigh data), polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.9 estimated), all other micronutrients estimated from USDA component data. 8. The dish is not halal-certified as standard yakitori tare contains mirin (alcohol-based) and the restaurants listed (Sukiya, Funan Mix & Match) are not confirmed halal-certified. 9. FoodOn URI FOODON_00002809 and FoodEx2 code A0CKP are used as closest parent matches; no exact ontology code exists for this composite dish.
